sentences.text: Well, as I said before, my father was a very gentle person and exceptional.
Now that I am old and can compare, he was very exceptionally patient and progressive in upbringing of his children.
When he used to take us to a park every-- every Sunday morning.
We went with him.
That was our outing.
Sunday morning, we went to park.
And as Europeans, we had the big meal at lunchtime.
And our live-in had the day off, so my mother cooked, which she most of the time cooked anyhow.
But we would go to a park.
And on the way home, there was a big conditori on the way home.
And we would go in and we could choose what we wanted.
And I remember those big boxes of these terrific full of calorie things.
In those days, nobody counted.
And we would bring a whole big box home.
So that was one part of my memories.

Then in-- in the later years, when I was more grown-up, shall we say, and we were more confined into the apartment when the Germans came, I used to play-- always every evening I used to play a card game with my father, which is called [NON-ENGLISH], which is like [NON-ENGLISH], only with two people and two card decks.
And actually, it is a very good thinking game.
And to this day, I play it with my husband.
And we enjoy it and you have got to think when you do play it.
And my sister tells me that she plays it with her grandchildren in Israel.
That's where she lives now.
The first major change was out of school.
That was the major change.
The second major change was the Star of David to be sewn on on your outer clothing.
And it had to be sewn on.
But then came the restrictions of no movies, curfew.
You were not allowed to go to places.
I can't really categorize them.
But I know we were not allowed to go there.
I don't even know if we were allowed to go on the trams, or only at certain times.
There came definite restrictions.
And also, there came restrictions of quarters of Prague.
Quarters were categorized.
That part of Prague, well, the people can keep their apartment, but they've got to take into the apartment which was occupied by-- by one family, they have got to take another two families.
So our apartment finished with two more families in the apartment.
Therefore, each one had one room.
And you had to get kind of--
No, not at all.
They were-- there was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they seemed old to me.
And one of the family, the lady was a very kind of-- she didn't like children, so we had to kind of cool it.
The other family I don't quite remember very well.
And-- and I guess they were OK.
No.
No children, because they measure it out by square footage or whatever footage they used in Europe in those days.
And so we were-- we had two families, man and a wife, and a man and a wife, no children.
Well, I wasn't quite informed.
They were there and that was it.
It wasn't a psychological discussion about it in the house, if that's what you mean, like-- like today [INAUDIBLE] take the children and-- and tell them god knows what about what.
It was, well, as of today, you have got to have a star on your closing and that's what it is.
Full stop.
I mean, there was no psychological preparedness for it of any kind.
I guess so.
I guess that's what it was.
I really cannot tell you.
I do not know.

Well, she just sewed the Star of David on my jacket and-- and I think we got three of them, so you sort of had to change them around as whatever you were wearing, so she was busy sewing the things on.
Oh, well, the instructions I think were not to be boisterous, not to-- if people don't want to talk to you-- and that meant my ex-school friends.
If they don't want to talk to you, you just keep on going.
Don't start saying hello if somebody crosses the street.
Don't say hello first.
Say hello if somebody says hello, this kind of thing.
And it-- she was right.
People, the ones who used to be my friends, used to cross the street if they saw me coming.
And there were very few who would actually go by and say very quietly, hello.
Being so young, I think it did not affect me very greatly.
I didn't-- you know, it-- it didn't.
No.
I don't think I-- I was very touched by the people avoiding me or the-- the whole scenario in that sense, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the youth would answer that question.
I think people who were much older would have felt, I'm sure, quite differently about it.
Well, as I said, the first year I had this private tutor, who was very patient with me.
And I did get some math and some geography and stuff.
But no, nothing very much.
What I know today is purely, as the saying goes, street-wise and from a lot of reading.
And I am a good observer and just picking up everything I possibly can.
My education is really a self-taught all around.
And my English, I am a-- like a-- I was-- I did not speak English and I wasn't taught English in a sense of any school.
I just was thrown, as they say, into the wolf's mouth and I had to learn.
And I did.
And my spelling to this day is not accurate and I'm not very sure of myself.

But I have found out throughout the years that there are doctors and-- and professors who went through universities and they do not know how to spell, so I do not feel so terrible about it as I used to many years ago.
Now, I will come to a-- yeah.
I will tell you about that in a moment.
But there was two episodes which I feel should be recorded.
We had two-- we-- the SS and the Gestapo, of course, would come into the building and raid the Jewish apartments.
Now, on one occasion, it was the Gestapo.
And our janitor downstairs, we had a-- sort of a signal and she would say Gestapo is in the building.
Sometimes they were in the building they didn't come to us, because there were other Jewish families in the-- in the building.
But one occasions they did come.
And there was stuff like chocolates, which neighbor used to bring us for us children.
She was working in a chocolate factory, and she would bring us boxes of chocolate.
And we had to eat it in a hurry, because that was a contraband.
And my father was a smoker and he had to flush the cigarettes down the toilet.
And they came and they were very like brutal, in a mannerism, brutal.
And-- and went through the apartment and, you know, threw everything about, and then stomped out.
But they did not take my father with them or anything like that, which that was on many occasion, I think on most occasions they did take the man of the-- of the house with them.
And then later on, either they'd return with a broken mouth or something, or didn't return at all.
Now, that did not happen to us.

The other time, which is actually a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] humorous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] story, the janitor called that somebody in a SS uniform, meaning an SS uniform, was asking for my father and is coming upstairs.
Well, the doorbell rang and there is this huge German standing there with the SS band and the uniform and the click.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was, you know, just as you imagine them and this they were.
And he says, oh, Anthony, how nice to see you.
And he threw his arms around him.
I thought, oh, my dear god.
Well, he was one of his-- my father's former client from the Sudetenland.
And he came into the living room and they chatted.

And I must tell you that it was a very, very uncomfortable feeling having this SS sitting there, all dolled out in his SS uniform, and he wanted to know if he could help Father with anything or any kind.
And I just was like, my god, what is he doing?
So that was kind of a humorous story.
I never heard of this man and I don't want to hear from this man.
But at least-- it-- it was beyond my comprehension that he would actually look up my father in a SS uniform and come.
I mean, that is-- I haven't heard a story like that from no one.
Were there food shortages?
Yes, there were food shortages.
But I really, you know, not-- not-- nothing drastic at that particular time.
I'm sure we had less meat.
And I'm sure we had less fruit.
And I'm sure we had less of-- less of stuff, but nothing which clicks my memory, no.
At that time?
Not really, no.
No.
Yes.
Well, the Germans-- I don't know how it came about and that would have to be by somebody who was much older than me, because he would know the ins and outs.
But at the real end of Prague, there was a playing complex.
I mean, it wasn't even a complex.
It was a field where there was a running track.
There was tennis courts, which are converted into volleyball courts, and a little wooden shack, which was operational.
And they used to have volunteers in it.
And in the summer, you could buy a lemonade there.
And in the winter, you could buy a hot chocolate there.
And what we did there, we congregated there.
And we ran track and we had gymnastic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] classes.
And the older kids played volleyball.
And that is the place where you finally-- well, I finally came into the Jewish environment of children, older and some younger but not much, and got quite a series of beautiful friendships.

And in the wintertime, we were skaters, so the parents or somebody flooded the volleyball, you know, field, and we skated there and then went into the hutch and had a hot chocolate and then we walked home.
And that was very good for us, because that was our-- our world, Hagibor, it was called.
And it was for that time-- time period, it was our world.

And it made us a part of a unit, which was starting to learn that being together and-- and friendship as such, not only a friendship of being in school, because that in the end I wouldn't call friendship.
That was real acquaintances.
This was starting the era in my life of deep connection, of friendships of completely different kind, and which lasted for a long time.
Unfortunately, most of the people which I was friends with died during the war.

But the point where-- where the feeling of togetherness and-- and loyalty and truthfulness started to mean something and was an honorable thing to apply, and that was where we were learning it in a way where we played volleyball.
The older ones played volleyball.
We were running track or doing gymnastics and somersaults or whatever.
Well, as far as I know, it lasted-- I don't-- well, when I left, it was still there.
Well, until I went to Theresienstadt, was called up for the transport.
Now, that was the Jewish community in Prague were under the SS or whomever they were reporting to.
And they were told that on this particular day so many people have got to go into a transport.
And now it was up-- as far as I understand, I might be wrong.
But they had to take the names out of a hat.
I mean, that was the whole thing.
Well, the first transport-- well, this was established.
Now I know that this was established.
And the first-- I think there were three transports, which went very early, in 1941, I think.
And they went to Warsaw.
And I-- I never found anybody from there.
There was particular one girl who was a very good friend of mine on the basis of friends as I described.
And their family went and I-- I have never heard of them.
And there were three transports, I think, two or three from Prague.
They didn't.
They didn't.
This is what is beyond of my imagination.
They didn't.
There was no resistance, not that there should-- there could have been, really.
There was no resistance.
But they went, like-- like sheep to the assembly center.
And then they were sent.
I think with the Warsaw one, I think there was.
But I'm not sure.
In the Theresienstadt one, when they start-- then there was a pause and there was nothing.
And then came the Theresienstadt ones.
And that, I think we knew where we were going.
I think-- yes.
We did.
Yes.
Well, it was an old-- well, it is and it was-- and they emptied for that particular reason.
It was an army village.
And it-- it had its borders, you know, like [INAUDIBLE] borders, we would call them today.
But they had like-- you couldn't escape, because they had borders all around the village.
And they are still there, those borders.
They were not established for the ghetto.
And I think that was one of the reasons they chose it.

And it was a closed ghetto, meaning that people were in their accommodation, working, taken by the-- by the military to-- to whatever they were working at, and then brought back into the-- into their accommodation.
But when I came to-- to Theresienstadt, it became an open ghetto, meaning that you could walk the streets to a certain time of the day.
There were-- as I said, the ghetto was naturally-- had a boundary.
But then, they had-- then the things like for a train, where the street, you could have walked out of, so that was guarded, of course.
And really, I think the Germans have established Theresienstadt as a-- as a showcase of-- of their [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] war [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ability of dealing with Jews.
We were notified about four days before, where we had to go and get to the assembly place.
And that was that.
Now, we are going back to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish community and the SS.

And you see, my father not being a able-bodied person, they said that my family has got to-- that two of us, like the girls have got to go and mother and father can stay in Prague for another period of time.
Nobody knew for how long.

And-- but because of the Jewish community, and they had some say in it I presume, they-- my mother objected, saying that, you know, she is not going to send two young girls away, and that she will go with me, because I was the youngest.
And my sister, who was 16 by then, would be a help to my father.
And she will stay with him.
And that's how it finished.
I went with my mother in July of 1942 to Theresienstadt.
And Father and my sister stayed in the apartment.
It must have been end of July.
Well, we were in a train.
We got to a station, like the train was packed and we had all kind of luggage and cooking and, you know, all kinds of things which we never saw.
It was a normal car.
It was a passenger car.
It was a passenger car, but it was stuffed, like stuffed full.
People were sitting on top of each other.
And it wasn't like when you go on a train.
I mean, we were now starting to experience the-- the crowding, OK?
Yes.
We were told that we can bring two suitcases and-- and-- and something or other, which we complied with, which we never saw.
Our transport didn't.
Some did, some didn't.
It-- it was just at ransom.
We didn't.
Our transport, my mother and me, we never saw anything we took out from Prague.
And we came to the station.
They got us out of the train onto some trucks.
And they drove us to Theresien.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, it was a shock, the shock of parting with Father and-- and my sister, and knowing that we are going to be separated, first in one's life not knowing what, where, and how.
And it was-- that was the shock.
But coming to Theresien, which was an open ghetto, they housed us in a-- in a small house with umpteen women in one room, Mother and me.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was scary, yes, because it's a new environment, which you don't know what to expect.
But as I have said before, if all the Jews-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, maybe I shouldn't phrase it like that.
If us and not take everybody-- if we could have finished the war in Theresienstadt, our family would have come out well.
The sanitary conditions were terrible.
They had toilets, but they didn't run most of the time and were stuck, because there was not enough plumbing.
There was lice.
There were fleas.
There was not-- you know, you could wash something in a-- in a sink.
There was water.
But there wasn't-- I mean, nothing.
I mean, it was like night and day.
Coming even from a crowded apartment in Prague, which was overcrowded, it still had a huge impact of the filth.
But again, on the outside of the streets and everything, it didn't look terrible terrible.
There was food.
You went into a kitchen, which you were-- like you were allotted to go to.
And you had a pot.
And they would give you some soup or some kind of a mush.
And that's what you had.
And that started the shortage of the food.
They gave you a piece of bread and so on and so forth.
Yes, people were working.
My mother was working in a laundry, which was outside the ghetto.
And she was escorted with a group of women outside the ghetto into the laundry, which was very hard work.
I was working in a-- in a land-- like digging around the plants and potatoes and stuff like that.
And again, that was outside the ghetto.
And we would be-- a group of youngsters would be escorted outside the ghetto and brought home.
No.
I was young and I was in a-- I was living with my mother only about three weeks in that particular accommodation.
She stayed there.
But as a child, I wasn't allowed to.
And I was-- I had to go to a children's place, which was one room.
Like it was a building, girls' building, L1011, I think.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were in a-- like there was rooms and rooms.
In each room, there was about 30 girls in bunks in that room.
And there would be a supervisor, a prisoner, same thing, only older one.
And she supervised that particular room.
I was very lucky.
We had a girl who must have been, oh, I don't know, 19, 20, something like that.
Her name was Irene, but we called her Uncle.
And she was the most delightful person there ever was.
And she made our life in the most terrible times the best she knew how.
She lifted us into a-- you know, she was exceptional, exceptional person.
And I today salute her, because she did return.
I saw her in Prague after the war.
I don't know what happened to her.
But to her, I salute, because she was an exceptional person.
Yes.
She was Jewish.
And she took us under her wing with the love and understanding completely.
Right.
